git_7

Mia Fitzgerald

3/3/2026

Filtering Color and Clarity

diamonds_clean <- diamonds %>%
  filter(color %in% c("D", "G", "J"), clarity %in% c("I1", "VS1", "IF"))

diamonds_clean %>%
  count(color, clarity) %>%
  kable()
color clarity n
D I1 42
D VS1 705
D IF 73
G I1 150
G VS1 2148
G IF 681
J I1 50
J VS1 542
J IF 51

I used the diamonds dataset to filter for the specific colors and clarities for the new dataset. I used the new dataset to count the combinations of them. G and VS1 were far above everything else. 1

Scatterplot of Price Versus Carat

diamonds_clean %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = carat, y = price)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")

The relationship between diamond price and weight is positive and linear. Meaning, as the weight of the diamond gets bigger, the more expensive it will be. 2

Incorporating Diamond Color

diamonds_clean %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = carat, y = price, color = color)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")

The diamond price of the color D tops the other colors in price. The colors G and J have a lower pricer per carat rate. 3

Incorporating Diamond Clarity

diamonds_clean %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = carat, y = price, color = color, shape = clarity)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(aes(linetype = clarity), method = "lm")

There is no clarity or readability of this plot due to the noise by all the different colors, shapes, and lines. I don’t think this visual is something someone wants to look at for more than a second. 4

Incorporating Color and Clarity Through Grid Facet

diamonds_clean %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = carat, y = price)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") + 
  facet_grid(color ~ clarity)

Adding the facet grid helped make out what specific combinations are lower or higher visually with regression lines. The color-clarity combination with the steepest relationship is D and IF. The graph also reflects how I1 is such a bad clarity. The J/G and I1 combination being the least steep. 5

Which Color-Clarity Combination has the Highest Price per Carat Ratio?

diamonds_clean %>% 
  mutate(ppc = price / carat) %>%
  group_by(color, clarity) %>%
  summarize(avg_ppc = mean(ppc)) %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_ppc)) %>%
  kable()
color clarity avg_ppc
D IF 9937.419
G VS1 4420.881
D VS1 4204.891
G IF 4007.010
J VS1 3750.351
J IF 3220.227
D I1 3064.485
J I1 2710.080
G I1 2617.478

The table does agree with my plots. It is just harder to make out when it isn’t expressed as a number. 6
